passageTitle: A Fairer Approach to Flexible Work
passage: Our revised flexibility policy should be treated as a practical way of organizing work, not as a reward for a favored few. Managers are expected to discuss remote days, adjusted start times, or compressed hours with any employee who makes a request. Approval is not automatic: reception, laboratory, and site duties may require physical cover. Even so, a role that cannot be fully remote may still allow some flexibility. I recommend judging arrangements by agreed results and service quality rather than by how often a manager sees someone at a desk. Teams must set common contact hours so colleagues and customers know when help is available. Every arrangement will be reviewed after three months, with changes made if workload has shifted unfairly. Flexible working should not reduce salary when contracted hours remain the same. Used carefully, the policy can improve retention without weakening accountability.
instructions: Do the following statements agree with the views or claims of the writer?

statements.text: The writer believes employees in on-site roles may still be offered some flexibility.
statements.evidence: A role that cannot be fully remote may still allow some flexibility.
statements.explanation: The statement directly agrees with the writer’s view that on-site duties do not exclude every flexible option.

statements.text: The writer thinks flexible working should be reserved for employees with exceptional performance.
statements.evidence: The policy should be treated as a practical way of organizing work, not as a reward for a favored few.
statements.explanation: The writer rejects treating flexibility as a special reward, so the statement contradicts the passage.

statements.text: The writer supports reviewing flexible arrangements after they have operated for a period.
statements.evidence: Every arrangement will be reviewed after three months.
statements.explanation: The statement agrees with the writer’s planned review of each arrangement.

statements.text: The writer says most staff prefer compressed hours to remote days.
statements.evidence: Several arrangements are listed, but no staff preference is reported.
statements.explanation: The passage gives no information about which option most employees prefer.

statements.text: The writer recommends evaluating flexible staff mainly by time visibly spent at a desk.
statements.evidence: I recommend judging arrangements by agreed results and service quality rather than by how often a manager sees someone at a desk.
statements.explanation: The statement proposes the exact measure the writer advises managers not to use.

statements.text: The writer believes the policy will reduce office rental costs within a year.
statements.evidence: Retention and accountability are discussed, but office costs are not mentioned.
statements.explanation: No view about rental savings or a one-year timeframe appears in the passage.

passageTitle: A Trial Garden for Mercer Street
passage: Some neighbors fear that turning the unused Mercer Street plot into a garden would remove all nearby parking. That objection overstates the proposal: four roadside spaces would remain, while only the fenced waste ground would change. I do not think the site should become a collection of private plots available only to experienced gardeners. Half the beds should support shared crops, and several raised beds should be kept for people who cannot work at ground level. A locked tool cupboard and short weekend demonstrations would help beginners take part without buying equipment. Water is a fair concern, so the plan includes roof-fed storage tanks rather than unrestricted use of the public supply. I recommend a twelve-month trial with open records of costs and participation. If too few residents use the space, the design can be reconsidered. Promising that the garden will solve every local problem would be foolish, but leaving the neglected plot unchanged offers no benefit either.
instructions: Do the following statements agree with the views or claims of the writer?

statements.text: The writer wants every garden bed to be assigned permanently to an experienced individual.
statements.evidence: I do not think the site should become a collection of private plots available only to experienced gardeners.
statements.explanation: The statement contradicts the writer’s support for shared crops and wider participation.

statements.text: The writer believes beginners should be able to join without purchasing their own tools.
statements.evidence: A locked tool cupboard and short weekend demonstrations would help beginners take part without buying equipment.
statements.explanation: The statement agrees with the writer’s practical proposal for shared tools.

statements.text: The writer says the storage tanks will hold enough water for two dry summers.
statements.evidence: Roof-fed storage tanks are proposed, but their capacity is not stated.
statements.explanation: The passage provides no information about how long stored water would last.

statements.text: The writer favors testing the garden before making the arrangement permanent.
statements.evidence: I recommend a twelve-month trial with open records of costs and participation.
statements.explanation: A fixed trial period is exactly the approach the writer recommends.

statements.text: The writer believes the garden will increase the value of nearby homes.
statements.evidence: The passage discusses access, water, parking, and participation, but not property values.
statements.explanation: No opinion about house prices is expressed.

statements.text: The writer claims the garden is guaranteed to solve all problems in the area.
statements.evidence: Promising that the garden will solve every local problem would be foolish.
statements.explanation: The statement directly contradicts the writer’s warning against unrealistic promises.

passageTitle: Why the North Loop Timetable Is Changing
passage: From Monday, North Loop buses will leave Central Square every twenty minutes instead of following the current uneven pattern. Some journeys will take three minutes longer because the route will stop beside the medical center. In my view, predictable service and useful access matter more than advertising the shortest possible travel time. Passengers should allow the new timetable two weeks to settle before deciding whether it works; early delays may reflect drivers and travelers learning the revised stops. I accept that moving the Market Street stop farther from the junction will inconvenience some older passengers. For that reason, seating and a covered shelter will be installed before the change begins. The council will examine punctuality data and passenger comments after six weeks. We will alter the schedule if connections repeatedly fail, but returning immediately to the old pattern would waste the chance to judge the new one fairly.
instructions: Do the following statements agree with the views or claims of the writer?

statements.text: The writer values a dependable timetable more than achieving the minimum journey time.
statements.evidence: Predictable service and useful access matter more than advertising the shortest possible travel time.
statements.explanation: The statement accurately reflects the writer’s stated priority.

statements.text: The writer believes the new medical-center stop will be used mainly by hospital employees.
statements.evidence: The medical-center stop is mentioned, but its expected users are not identified.
statements.explanation: The writer gives no view about which passenger group will use that stop most.

statements.text: The writer thinks passengers should judge the revised service after its first day.
statements.evidence: Passengers should allow the new timetable two weeks to settle before deciding whether it works.
statements.explanation: The statement contradicts the writer’s recommendation to wait before evaluating the service.

statements.text: The writer recognizes that relocating one stop may create difficulty for older passengers.
statements.evidence: I accept that moving the Market Street stop farther from the junction will inconvenience some older passengers.
statements.explanation: The writer explicitly acknowledges this disadvantage.

statements.text: The writer wants the old timetable restored as soon as any connection is missed.
statements.evidence: Returning immediately to the old pattern would waste the chance to judge the new one fairly.
statements.explanation: The writer argues against an immediate reversal, so the statement contradicts the notice.

statements.text: The writer says the timetable change will reduce council spending on fuel.
statements.evidence: The notice discusses timing, access, shelters, and evaluation, but not fuel costs.
statements.explanation: No claim about fuel savings is made.

passageTitle: Choosing a Course You Can Finish
passage: Adults returning to study often ask which course looks most impressive. I suggest beginning with a different question: which format can you continue when work or family life becomes busy? An intensive course is not automatically the best choice simply because it ends sooner. For some learners, a weekly class creates a steadier routine; others need recorded lessons they can pause. Before paying, attend a trial session or ask to see a sample unit. A recognized certificate matters when an employer or professional body requires it, but collecting qualifications without a clear purpose can waste both time and money. Online study is not the easy option people sometimes imagine. It replaces travel with the need to plan regular study periods and seek help early. Choose a course that demands effort, certainly, but not one whose timetable makes completion unlikely from the start.
instructions: Do the following statements agree with the views or claims of the writer?

statements.text: The writer believes the quickest course is always the most suitable option for an adult learner.
statements.evidence: An intensive course is not automatically the best choice simply because it ends sooner.
statements.explanation: The absolute claim contradicts the writer’s warning that speed alone does not determine suitability.

statements.text: The writer advises learners to inspect some course material before making payment.
statements.evidence: Before paying, attend a trial session or ask to see a sample unit.
statements.explanation: The statement agrees with the writer’s recommendation to preview the learning experience.

statements.text: The writer thinks online courses require learners to manage their study time actively.
statements.evidence: Online study replaces travel with the need to plan regular study periods and seek help early.
statements.explanation: This directly supports the statement about active time management.

statements.text: The writer says evening classes have higher completion rates than weekend classes.
statements.evidence: Weekly and recorded formats are discussed, but completion rates for evening and weekend classes are not compared.
statements.explanation: The passage contains no evidence for this comparison.

statements.text: The writer recommends collecting as many qualifications as possible, even without a specific goal.
statements.evidence: Collecting qualifications without a clear purpose can waste both time and money.
statements.explanation: The writer explicitly warns against the behavior recommended in the statement.

statements.text: The writer believes employers should pay the full cost of all recognized courses.
statements.evidence: Employer requirements are mentioned, but responsibility for course fees is not discussed.
statements.explanation: No opinion is given about who should pay.

passage: A good rental inspection is a planned check on maintenance, not an opportunity for an owner to search through a tenant’s private life. Written notice should explain the date, approximate time, and purpose of the visit. I encourage tenants to attend if convenient because questions can be resolved immediately, but their absence should not be treated as permission to open cupboards or personal containers. Photographs are useful for recording a leaking wall or damaged fitting; wide images of unrelated belongings should be avoided. Inspectors must also distinguish ordinary clutter from damage to the property. A blocked fire exit or exposed wire requires prompt action, whereas an untidy desk does not. After the visit, both sides should receive the same short written record, including agreed repair dates. This approach may feel formal, but clear boundaries protect responsible owners and tenants alike.
instructions: Do the following statements agree with the views or claims of the writer?

statements.text: The writer believes the purpose of an inspection should be stated before the visit.
statements.evidence: Written notice should explain the date, approximate time, and purpose of the visit.
statements.explanation: The statement directly agrees with the writer’s notice recommendation.

statements.text: The writer thinks an absent tenant gives an inspector permission to examine personal containers.
statements.evidence: Their absence should not be treated as permission to open cupboards or personal containers.
statements.explanation: The statement directly contradicts the boundary set by the writer.

statements.text: The writer says most inspection disagreements involve photographs of bedrooms.
statements.evidence: Photography is discussed, but the frequency and location of disputes are not reported.
statements.explanation: The passage gives no information about the most common source of disagreement.

statements.text: The writer considers a safety hazard more urgent than an untidy workspace.
statements.evidence: A blocked fire exit or exposed wire requires prompt action, whereas an untidy desk does not.
statements.explanation: The writer clearly distinguishes urgent safety risks from harmless untidiness.

statements.text: The writer believes tenants should arrange all repairs themselves after an inspection.
statements.evidence: The passage calls for agreed repair dates but does not assign every repair to the tenant.
statements.explanation: Responsibility for all repairs is not specified.

statements.text: The writer regards written post-inspection records as unnecessary formality.
statements.evidence: Both sides should receive the same short written record, and clear boundaries protect owners and tenants.
statements.explanation: The writer supports written records despite their formality, contradicting the statement.

passageTitle: What We Ask of Riverside Volunteers
passage: Riverside Week succeeds because volunteers return when they say they will. Previous event experience is welcome, but I value reliability and willingness to ask questions more highly. Every volunteer must attend one orientation, where team leaders explain safety, accessibility, and whom to contact when a problem arises. Please do not accept a task that feels unsafe merely to avoid disappointing someone; tell your leader so another role can be found. We cannot promise that everyone will receive a first-choice position. However, after the opening shift, volunteers may request a change if another team has space. Local bus fares can be reimbursed with a receipt, while taxi costs require approval before travel. At the final meeting, I want honest suggestions, including criticism. Praise alone will not help us make next year’s program more welcoming or easier to run.
instructions: Do the following statements agree with the views or claims of the writer?

statements.text: The writer values dependable attendance more than previous volunteering experience.
statements.evidence: Previous event experience is welcome, but I value reliability and willingness to ask questions more highly.
statements.explanation: The statement matches the writer’s explicit ranking of volunteer qualities.

statements.text: The writer says the orientation will last exactly two hours.
statements.evidence: Orientation is compulsory, but its duration is not stated.
statements.explanation: No length is given for the orientation session.

statements.text: The writer believes volunteers should continue with any task even when it feels unsafe.
statements.evidence: Please do not accept a task that feels unsafe; tell your leader so another role can be found.
statements.explanation: The statement contradicts the writer’s safety instruction.

statements.text: The writer guarantees every volunteer the role they request first.
statements.evidence: We cannot promise that everyone will receive a first-choice position.
statements.explanation: The guarantee in the statement is explicitly denied.

statements.text: The writer welcomes critical comments about how the program operates.
statements.evidence: I want honest suggestions, including criticism.
statements.explanation: The writer directly invites criticism as part of improving the program.

statements.text: The writer believes volunteers from outside the area are less committed than local volunteers.
statements.evidence: Travel reimbursement is discussed, but local and non-local commitment is not compared.
statements.explanation: The writer expresses no opinion about commitment based on where volunteers live.
